7 min read
8 hours ago

--

“Your Angular App Becomes Confusing” usually shows up right after your first “real” feature lands. Not the tutorial feature where you build a list and a detail view. The one where product asks for three small changes that touch five screens, a modal, and a shared header.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Everything still works. Tests might even be green. Yet you start feeling something worse than a red error: you do not know where the truth lives anymore.
This usually looks harmless at the beginning. You pass an @Input() down a couple levels, listen to an @Output(), maybe grab a ViewChild to “quickly” call a method, and now two siblings coordinate through their parent. You ship. You feel productive.
Then you open the code two weeks later and you realize you need a whiteboard to answer a basic question: “What happens when the user clicks Save?”
Why this problem matters in real applications
Real applications do not fail from lack of features. They fail from change. “Add a badge.” “Allow draft mode.” “Support undo.” “Show warnings from the server.”
When components talk too much, each change forces you to touch many files, and each touch creates a new chance to break something unrelated. Debugging becomes…
